sections.java: // Implement AutoCloseable for try-with-resources class Span implements AutoCloseable { private final long start = System.nanoTime(); @Override public void close() { System.out.println(System.nanoTime() - start); } } try (Span s = new Span()) { doWork(); }
sections.python: # Implement __enter__ / __exit__ class Span: def __enter__(self): self.start = time.perf_counter() return self # bound to 'as' target def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc, tb): print(time.perf_counter() - self.start) return False # False = don't swallow errors with Span(): do_work()

sections.title: __exit__ Receives the Exception — and Can Suppress It
sections.content: __exit__(self, exc_type, exc, tb) always runs, like a finally — but it also sees any exception that escaped the block. Return False (or None) to let it propagate; return True to swallow it. Java's close() can't do that. Forgetting and accidentally returning True silently hides bugs, so be deliberate.

sections.java: // No lightweight option — a full class // even for a tiny timer. class Timer implements AutoCloseable { final String label; final long start = System.nanoTime(); Timer(String label) { this.label = label; } @Override public void close() { System.out.println(label + ": " + (System.nanoTime() - start)); } } try (var t = new Timer("load")) { doWork(); }
sections.python: # @contextmanager: one generator, no class from contextlib import contextmanager @contextmanager def timer(label: str): start = time.perf_counter() try: yield # <-- the 'with' body runs here finally: print(f"{label}: {time.perf_counter()-start:.3f}s") with timer("load"): do_work()

sections.title: @contextmanager Turns a Generator Into a Resource
sections.content: Everything before yield is setup, everything after (ideally in a finally) is teardown, and the yielded value becomes the as target. It's the fastest way to write a context manager — no class, no dunder methods. The stdlib also ships ready-made ones: contextlib.suppress(FileNotFoundError) (cleanly ignore an exception), closing(obj) (call .close()), and ExitStack (manage a dynamic number of with-resources).

sections.question: In a context manager's __exit__, what does returning True do?
options: Suppresses (swallows) any exception raised inside the with block
Nothing — the return value is ignored
sections.explanation: __exit__ receives the active exception (if any) and its return value decides its fate: truthy suppresses it, falsy (False/None) lets it propagate. Java's AutoCloseable.close() has no such power — it can't decide whether the original exception survives.
